coredata.dc:title: High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots: green synthesis and application as “off-on” fluorescent sensors for the determination of Fe3+ and adenosine triphosphate in biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Qitong
Li, Qian
Chen, Yuanfang
Tong, Lili
Lin, Xiaofeng
Zhu, Jieji
Tong, Qingxiao
coredata.dc:description: In this work, for the first time, natural bauhinia flower was adopted as a carbon source to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through facile and green microwave method without further passivation or modification. The fluorescent quantum yield (QY = 27.00%) of the as-synthesized N-CDs was higher than most of CDs prepared from other natural sources. The N-CDs was proved to be an effective “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for detection of Fe3+ exhibiting excellent sensitivity with the detection limit of 0.01 μM. The fluorescence of N-CDs-Fe3+ system would be recovered when ATP was added mainly due to high affinity of the adenosine triphosphate (ATP) for Fe3+ through Fe-O-P bonds. Therefore, a sensitive and reliable “turn-on” fluorescent ATP sensor was developed, the limit detection was calculated to be 0.005 μM. In addition, the fluorescent sensors could act as a good candidate to determine Fe3+ and ATP in biological samples with satisfying results. What’s more, the application of the fluorescent sensors for imaging Fe3+ and ATP in living cells, which suggested that the systems had great potential applications in biosensing and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ferric ion
Bauhinia flower
Fluorescent sensor
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and ATP
Synthesis and morphology characterization of N-CDs
Intracellular imaging of Fe3+ and ATP
Detection of Fe3+ and ATP in water or human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supportedby the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51673113 ), the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program No. 2013CB834803), the Education Bureau of Fujian Province (Project No. JAT160875), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhangzhou (Project No. ZZ2016J31 and ZZ2017J20) and the Training Program Foundation for Excellent Youth Researching Talents of Fujian’s Universities (Fujian Education Section, No. 2016-23).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One-pot green synthesis of high quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a “off-on” fluorescent sensor for determination of Fe3+ and ATP in biological and water samples (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The TEM image(a) and size distributions(b) for N-CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of N-CDs. The XPS spectra of N-CDs (d), C1 s (e) and N1 s (f).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs. (b) Excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. (c) Fluorescent spectra changes with various excitation wavelength. I0 represents the fluorescent intensity at 442 nm with 355 nm as excitation wavelength and I represents measured values. (d) Fluorescent spectra for N-CDs and N-CDs/Fe3+, the concentration of Fe3+ is 250 μM.

(a) Fluorescent spectra changes of the system upon addition of various amount of ferric ions. The concentrations of ferric ions from a to o are 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500 μM, respectively. (b) Plot of I/I0 as a function with the ferric ions concentration in 0–40 μM and 40–350 μM. (Conditions: 2.00 mL 5.75 mg mL−1 N-CDs, λex = 355 nm).

(a) Fluorescent spectra changes of the system upon addition of ATP. The concentrations of ATP from a to o are 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450 μM, respectively. (b) Plot of I’/I’0 as a function with the ATP concentration in 0.01 μM–450 μM. (Conditions: 2.00 mL 5.75 mg mL−1 N-CDs and 600 μM Fe3+, λex = 355 nm).

Fluorescence microscopy images of Hela cells: (a) incubated with 5.75 mg mL−1 N-CDs for 6 h at 37 °C, (b) fluorescence image of N-CDs treated cells after incubation with 300 μM Fe3+ for 20 min at 37 °C, (c) fluorescence image after further incubation with 300 μM ATP for 30 min at 37 °C.

head.ce:title: High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots: green synthesis and application as “off-on” fluorescent sensors for the determination of Fe3+ and adenosine triphosphate in biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Key Laboratory for Preparation and Application of Ordered Structural Materials of Guangdong Province, Shantou University, Guangdong 515063, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Key Laboratory for Preparation and Application of Ordered Structural Materials of Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Gannan Medical University, Ganzhou, 341000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Engineering Research Center of Pesticide and Medicine Intermediate Clean Production, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes, Ministry of Education, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Pesticide and Medicine Intermediate Clean Production
Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, natural bauhinia flower was adopted as a carbon source to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through facile and green microwave method without further passivation or modification. The fluorescent quantum yield (QY = 27.00%) of the as-synthesized N-CDs was higher than most of CDs prepared from other natural sources. The N-CDs was proved to be an effective “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for detection of Fe3+ exhibiting excellent sensitivity with the detection limit of 0.01 μM. The fluorescence of N-CDs-Fe3+ system would be recovered when ATP was added mainly due to high affinity of the adenosine triphosphate (ATP) for Fe3+ through Fe-O-P bonds. Therefore, a sensitive and reliable “turn-on” fluorescent ATP sensor was developed, the limit detection was calculated to be 0.005 μM. In addition, the fluorescent sensors could act as a good candidate to determine Fe3+ and ATP in biological samples with satisfying results. What’s more, the application of the fluorescent sensors for imaging Fe3+ and ATP in living cells, which suggested that the systems had great potential applications in biosensing and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ion (Fe3+), one of the most important transition metal ions in our daily life, plays significant roles in environmental science and clinical medicine [1,2]. Maintaining the balance of Fe3+ ions in our body could prevent many diseases [3,4], such as neurological function disorders, iron-deficiency anemia, diabetes, heart failure, intelligence decline, hypoimmunity, low productivity and weak anti-infection ability. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ is essential for the early diagnosis and monitoring of disease. Acting as an extracellular and intracellular signal molecule, Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is crucial for the metabolic processes and provides an energy source for daily activities [5,6]. The concentration of ATP is associated with many diseases, such as ngiocardiopathy, malignant tumors, ischemia, Parkinson's disease, angiocardiopathy, et al [7,8]. Hence, quantitative determination of ATP is a subject of great importance in biochemical study.

As well known, fluorescent sensors have attracted much attention due to its distinct advantages such as high selectivity, high sensitivity, rapid response, operational simplicity and real-time monitoring [9–21]. Thence, fluorescent sensors have attracted wide attention for the determination of Fe3+ [11–15] and ATP [5–7,16,10–21]. Thus, Many materials were prepared as luminescence reagents or modificative agent for quantification of Fe3+ or ATP. Although most of them had made many contributions to detection of Fe3+ or ATP, these materials either exhibited limited sensitivity or they involved sophisticated and expensive syntheses.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have been extensively studied due to their excellent water solubility, anti-photobleaching, chemical stability, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility compared to conventionally fluorescent chromophores based on organic small molecules (OSMs) and Metal semiconductor quantum dots (MSQDs) [22–28]. They are considered to be attractive nanomaterials with great potential applications in various fields including fluorescent sensor, bio-imaging, photocatalysis, solar cells, light-emitting diodes, photodynamic therapy and drug delivery [29–31]. In the past few years, a variety of raw materials were used as precursors for the fabrication of CDs, among which natural materials were one of the most popular materials. Natural materials are easily available in nature compared to general chemicals, which is beneficial to the large-scale production and industrialized application of CDs. In addition, natural materials usually contain multifarious hetero atoms, so CDs prepared from these materials are always packed with various surface groups and possess unique properties without further passivation or modification. Therefore, many efforts have been devoted to synthesize CDs from various natural biomass such as rose-heart radish [15], tomato juice [32], cabbage [33] fish scales [34], solanum tuberosum [35], cornflour [36], punica granatum fruits [37], apple juice [38], lotus root [39], ocimum sanctum [40], sweet potato [41], etc. Although great achievements have been obtained, there are still some weakness that have not been completely overcome yet. For instance, the reported CDs based on natural products mostly show low fluorescent quantum yield (QY), generally less than 10% [34–41]. Besides, some CDs from natural materials have fatal defect that is the poor selectivity as fluorescent sensor. Accordingly, it is desperately needed to prepare natural materials-based CDs with high QY and selectivity by a simple, green and cost-effective strategy.

In this work, for the first time, natural bauhinia flower was adopted as precursors to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through facile and green microwave method without any passivators or additives as shown in Fig. 1 . The N-CDs was proved to be an effective “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for detection of Fe3+ and exhibited excellent sensitivity, selectivity, quick response, biocompatibility, fluorescence stability with temperature and pH change. The fluorescence of N-CDs-Fe3+ system would be recovered when ATP was added. Therefore, a sensitive and reliable “turn-on” fluorescent ATP sensor was developed. In addition, this fluorescent sensor could act as a good candidate to determine Fe3+ and ATP in biological samples with satisfying results.
The reagents and apparatus were shown in the Supplementary information.

10.0 g dry bauhinia flower was added into 50 mL mixed solution of ethanol and H2O (1:1, v/v) and stirred rapidly with an electric beater. After 30 min, the mixture was placed into the microwave oven and irradiated microwave at 1000 W for 10 min. Cooling down to ambient temperature, the mixture was centrifugated at 5000 rpm for 30 min and then the supernate was dialyzed through dialysis membranes (Molecular Weight Cut Off (MWCO) = 1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the resulting solution was freeze-dried to give N-CDs as an orange-yellow solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and ATP
ce:section.ce:para: 2.00 mL 5.75 mg mL−1 of N-CDs were placed in a cuvette, following by the addition of a calculated amount of Fe3+. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with excitation wavelength of 355 nm at room temperature. For detection of ATP, 500 μM of Fe3+ was added to 2.00 mL 5.75 mg mL−1 of N-CDs to form a mixed solution. Then different amounts of ATP were added to N-CDs-Fe3+ system. The fluorescence spectra were present under excitation at 355 nm at room temperature.

The QY was measured according to the method previously reported by comparing the as-prepared N-CDs with the reference quinine sulfate of their absorbency values and integrated photoluminescence intensities [42]. The quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution and the CDs was dissolved in distilled water.
Φ =
Φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
Where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index, and A is the optical density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and morphology characterization of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The N-CDs was prepared using bauhinia flower as the only raw material via a simple microwave-assisted method at 1000 W for 10 min. The morphology and surface functional groups characterization were carried out through TEM, FTIR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2
a and Fig. 2b, the as-prepared N-CDs was approximately spherical in shape, finely-dispersed and possessed a homogeneous morphology and narrow size distribution between 2 and 6 nm with an average diameter of 3.4 nm. The FTIR spectrum of the N-CDs was given in Fig. 2 c. The absorption bands at 3316 cm−1 and 3166 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH2, respectively. The band at 1619 cm-1 aroused from the stretching vibrations of CO and aromatic CN heterocycles. The peak at 1472 cm−1 was attributed to −COO- and NH group [43,44]. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) measurements were also performed to make further characterization of N-CDs nanoparticles (Fig. 2d). The peaks of C 1 s and N 1 s of the N-CDs were shown in Fig. 2 (e) and (f), respectively. The C 1s peaks appeared at 284.5 eV, 285.2 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.0 eV, which was attributed to the CC/CC, CN, CO and CO bonds, respectively [45]. The decomposition of N1 s spectra into two peaks demonstrated that the presence of two different types of nitrogen. The peaks at 398.8 and 401.1 eV could be assigned to C = NC and CNH2, respectively [46]. The XPS spectrum was in a good agreement with FTIR spectrum, which indicated that the hydrophilic group was rich in N-CDs. This is also well consistent with the good water solubility of N-CDs.

To investigate the optical properties, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies of N-CDs in aqueous solution were recorded. The UV–vis spectrums (Fig. 3 a) clearly showed two absorption peaks at 228 nm and 282 nm, respectively. The absorption peak at 228 nm is due to π-π* transition of CC, and the absorption at 282 nm corresponds to n-π* transition of the CO bond [44,47]. The excitation and emission spectra were shown in Fig. 3b, the maximum emission wavelength was located at 442 nm with the corresponding optimal excitation wavelength at 355 nm. Using quinine sulfate as reference, the QY of the N-CDs was calculated to be up to 27.00%, which is higher than most of CDs prepared from other natural sources (Table S1). As we all know, bauhinia flower mainly consists of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen elements, Thus, we expect this natural material to be a promising precursor for synthesizing N-CDs. The N-CDs emit bright blue fluorescence with a high QY due to the fluorescence enhancement effect of N dopant atoms on a surface of CDs, which can be demonstrated convincingly by the analysis of XPS results [48,49]. Like lots of CDs reported previously, the N-CDs exhibited typical excitation-dependent fluorescent emission character as depicted in Fig. 3 c. The fluorescent emission peak position gradually red-shifted with the excitation wavelength progressively increasing from 295 nm to 385 nm. The fluorescent intensity of emission peaks firstly climbed up and then declined, and fluorescent intensity reached the maximum value with the excitation wavelength at 355 nm. As shown in Fig. 3d, it was obvious that the fluorescent intensity sharply decreased after adding Fe3+. The strong fluorescence-quenching effect was possibly because of −OH, -NH2 and COOH distribution on the surface of N-CDs chelated or coordinated with Fe3+, which made the excited state electrons of N-CDs transfer to the unoccupied 3d orbital of Fe3+ and led to non-radiative electron/hole recombination [50].

To explore the response speed of N-CDs-based fluorescent sensor, we performed wavelength scan (Fig. S1a) and time scan (Fig. S1b) after Fe3+ was added into N-CDs aqueous solution, respectively. The results proved that fluorescent intensity immediately reduced once Fe3+ was added and remained almost unchanged with longer reaction time. Therefore, instant detection was carried out in the subsequent tests. To evaluate fluorescent stability of N-CDs, fluorescence intensity was determined under various temperature and pH conditions were shown in Fig. S1c and Fig. S1d. The fluorescent intensity changed inconspicuously with temperature range from 20 °C to 70 °C (Fig. S1c) indicating that the structure of N-CDs was quite stable and not readily disrupted with environmental temperature variation. The intensity was essentially stable under moderate alkaline or acidic condition of pH 4–8 (Fig. S1d). The −OH and −COOH on the surface of N-CDs was protonated in strong acid environment. If the content of OH− was sufficient, Fe3+ would be hydrolyzed to generate Fe(OH)3 precipitate and self-polymerization of N-CDs may be formed [51]. All the above situations would reduce the fluorescent intensity of the system. It was demonstrated that the pH value had a certain influence on fluorescent intensity. Meanwhile, for the determination of ATP, the response time and fluorescent stability (temperature and pH) was also explained. Fig. S2a showed the response time was very quickly. At the same time, with the increase of reaction time, the fluorescent intensity of the system had hardly changed. Fig. S2b indicated the system has excellent temperature stability with temperature range from 20 °C to 70 °C. As shown in Fig. S2c, when the value of pH is range from 5 to 8, the fluorescent intensity of the system was strongest. Due to the pH of the biological sample is slightly neutral, we chose the pH = 7 as the best condition. In general, the fast response characteristics and good fluorescent stability benefit the practical application of the N-CDs.

In order to understand the binding features of N-CDs with different metals ions, we investigated the response behaviors of a series of metal ions which include Fe3+, Cu2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+, as shown in Fig. S3. Under the same concentration, the N-CDs manifested specific recognition towards Fe3+, while other competitive metal cations exerted an almost negligible influence. It has been reported that Fe3+ shows higher affinity with nitrogen and oxygen atom contained in N-CDs, which would promote the coordination reaction between N-CDs and Fe3+ and further make the electron transfer from N-CDs to Fe3+ and finally cause fluorescence quenching. We also studied the interference effect of coexisting metal cations by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 442 nm in the presence of excessive other possible interfering ions. These results attested the remarkable selectivity and anti-interference ability of the as-prepared N-CDs for Fe3+ detection.

In addition, the sensitivity was explored for N-CDs sensing Fe3+, by which the working curve, linear range and lowest limit detection were acquired. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the fluorescent intensity at 442 nm decreased progressively with the Fe3+ concentration increasing little by little between 10 and 350 μM. It presented two good linear calibration curves between I/I0 and c Fe 3+ in two concentration ranges of 0–40 μM and 40–350 μM with lowest limit detection of 0.01 μM (3σ), which showed this probe has higher sensitivity than other probes of Fe3+ reported previously [12,49,50,52–56] (Table S2). I and I0 represent the fluorescent intensity in presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively. The linear equation was I/I0 = 0.9973 - 0.0062 c Fe 3+ (0–40 μM, R = 0.9936) and I/I0 = 0.8206 - 0.0019 c Fe 3+ (40–350 μM, R = 0.9928), respectively. The wide linear range and low limit detection indicated that the as-prepared N-CDs could be deemed as an excellent fluorescent sensor. It's worth noting that the N-CDs was synthesized through a simple, green and economical microwave-assisted method without further surface modification or passivation. The N-CDs is applicable candidates to be employed for recognition and detection of Fe3+.

ATP could be expected to act as a complexing agent for Fe3+, because ATP had a high affinity for Fe3+ through Fe-O-P bonds. To ensure the N-CDs-Fe3+ system could be used for sensitive determination of ATP, the fluorescence responses induced by ATP at different concentrations were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Fe3+ was strengthened with the ATP’s concentration increase. At the same time, Fig. 5 b showed that a liner relationship between I’/I’0 (I’ and I’0 represent the fluorescent intensity in presence and absence of ATP in the N-CDs-Fe3+ system, respectively) and ATP’s concentration ranged from 0.01 μM to 450 μM with the regression equation was I’/I’0 = 1.3131 - 0.0117 cATP (R = 0.9978). The limit detection was calculated to be 0.005 μM, which showed higher sensitivity than other ATP fluorescent sensor [5,16–21] (Table S3). All of these results revealed that N-CDs-Fe3+ system could be used as an excellent “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for ATP.

As the proof-of-concept, other biological molecules, including guanosine 5′-triphosphate (GTP), cytosine 5′-triphosphate (CTP), uridine 5′-triphosphate (UTP), dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) were investigated in the system. As shown in Fig. S4, only ATP could restore the fluorescence intensity, while almost other substances could hardly make the fluorescence intensity change, even at higher concentration (1 mM) compared to ATP (100 μM). The results indicated this system exhibited good selectivity for ATP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging of Fe3+ and ATP
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular cytotoxicity of N-CDs toward HeLa cells were performed in the Methylthiazolyldiphenyl- tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay before biological application. As shown in Fig. S5, when the concentration of N-CDs was 5 mg/L, the cell viability is still over 98% after 36 h. Moreover, a favorable cell viability of >95% was still maintained at a high concentration of N-CDs up to 500 mg/L, which indicated that the N-CDs could act as a good bioimaging reagent. Fig. 6 showed fluorescent images of intracellular imaging in HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. 6a, HeLa cells could incubate with N-CDs and showed strong blue fluorescence. After 300 μM Fe3+ was added in the above nutrient solution for 20 min at 37 °C, it exhibited very weak fluorescence (Fig. 6b). However, when 300 μM ATP was added in the above system for 30 min at 37 °C, the fluorescence of the system was recovered (Fig. 6c). All above results indicated that the “off-on” fluorescent sensor could be used in semiquantitative imaging intracellular Fe3+ and ATP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and ATP in water or human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the validity and applicability of the “off-on” fluorescent sensor, the as-synthesized N-CDs was used to determine Fe3+ and ATP in water samples or human serum. As shown in Tables S4 and S5, the recovery rates of the samples ranged between 99.00% and 103.50%, and all results were in agreement with inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (The determination of Fe3+, Table S4) and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (The determination of ATP, Table S5), which indicated that some other substances could not interfere with the determination of Fe3+ and ATP. Therefore, the proposed method could be effectively used for the direct determination of Fe3+ and ATP in water or human serum samples.

We developed a facile and eco-friendly one-step microwave-assisted approach to fabricate a monodisperse N-CDs by using bauhinia flower as only precursor. The N-CDs was proved to be an effective “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for detection of Fe3+ with excellent sensitivity, selectivity, quick response, biocompatibility. A sensitive and reliable “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for ATP was also developed based on the N-CDs-Fe3+ system. Meanwhile, the as-synthesized N-CDs could be applied for determination of Fe3+ and ATP in biological samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supportedby the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51673113), the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program No. 2013CB834803), the Education Bureau of Fujian Province (Project No. JAT160875), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhangzhou (Project No. ZZ2016J31 and ZZ2017J20) and the Training Program Foundation for Excellent Youth Researching Talents of Fujian’s Universities (Fujian Education Section, No. 2016-23).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.089.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Biosens. Bioelectron.
A new fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dot system modified by the fluorophore-labeled ssDNA for the analysis of 6-mercaptopurine and Hg (II)
Biosens. Bioelectron.
Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Chem. Mater.
Synthesis of nitrogen-doping carbon dots with different photoluminescence properties by controlling the surface states
Nanoscale
Facilely synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots with high fluorescent yield for sensing Fe3+
New J. Chem.
Preparation of highly photoluminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots for Fe (III) detection
J. Mater. Chem. A
Understanding the selective detection of Fe3+ based on graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probes: the Ksp of a metal hydroxide-assisted mechanism
Anal. Chem.
An ionic liquid promoted microwave-hydrothermal route towards highly photoluminescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of iron (III)
RSC Adv.
Fluorescence quenchometric method for determination of ferric ion using boron-doped carbon dots
Microchim. Acta
One-pot solid phase pyrolysis synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging
Sens. Actuators B Chem.
Highly Fe3+-selective fluorescent nanoprobe based on ultrabright N/P codoped carbon dots and its application in biological samples
Anal. Chem.
One-step synthesis of self-doped carbon dots with highly photoluminescence as multifunctional biosensors for detection of iron ions and pH
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qitong Huang received his B.S. degree from Minnan Normal University in 2014. Now, He is currently a PhD student in Shantou University. His research interests include electrochemical sensors and fluorescent sensors for detection of biological molecules and metal ions.
Qian Li received her B.S. degree from Shantou University in 2017. Her current research interests include carbon dots-based fluorescent sensors for detection of metal ions.
Yuanfang Chen is currently a B.S. student in Shantou University. Her current research interests mainly focus on chemical biology.
Lili Tong is a lecturer in Shandong Normal University, Her current research interests include fluorescent sensors for detection of biological molecules.

Xiaofeng Lin received his B.S. degree from Minnan Normal University in 2014. Now, She is currently a PhD student in Shantou University. Her current research interests mainly focus on chemical biology.
Jieji Zhu received his B.S. degree from Shantou University in 2014. Now, He is currently a PhD student in Shantou University. He current research interests mainly focus on chemical biology.
Qingxiao Tong is a professor in Shantou University, his research interests cover nanoscience, organic chemistry and biochemistry.
